title: BATTLE OF THE ROCKETS 2026
what: Designed, built, and launched two high-power rockets for the 2026 Battle of the Rockets (FGE): Mars Rover delivery vehicle (79.125 in, 19.30 lb, AeroTech J540R-14, 6.08 in OD, 1,000–2,000 ft target) and Altitude Rocket “Blue Phenix Jr” (42.75 in, F42-8T, tube-fin only, 801 ft target) — both cleared PDR/CDR milestones, compliant with FGE, NAR, and FAA

Both vehicles underwent full design iteration PDR→CDR: Mars Rover motor swapped (Loki J525-LW → J540R-14), fins redesigned (4-fin trapezoidal → 3-fin PETG-CF), length grown 75→79.125 in, mass reduced 20.58→19.30 lb, T/W raised 5.97→6.85:1; Altitude rocket tube fins doubled (3→6), airframe swapped (Blue Tube → phenolic cardboard), length reduced 49→42.75 in, parachute cut 36→24 in, T/W 11.03:1

Ongoing aerodynamic modeling study (AeroSim-Stability-Subsonic) for GNC integration — characterizing Cₗ, Cᴅ, Cₘ across Mach 0.1–0.7, AoA 0–10°, and sideslip 0–10° via steady-state RANS sweeps; applying NASA TM X-195 energy management framework to quantify drag-area-to-mass effects on apogee and recovery timing
how: Aerodynamic design: full vehicle CAD in SolidWorks; RockSim stability sweeps targeting 2.0–2.5 cal static margin; Plastalina clay deadweight added aft on Mars Rover to optimize CG; Altitude deadweight repositioned front→rear; PETG nose cone retention mechanism redesigned with threaded interface; both vehicles reached ≥ 5:1 T/W

CFD (ongoing): rhoSimpleFoam steady-state compressible RANS with Spalart-Allmaras turbulence (OpenFOAM v2412); blockMesh + snappyHexMesh domain (19.43 × 9.92 × 9.92 m, base 210×120×110 cells, Level 7 feature + wake refinement); Mach/α/β swept via inlet velocity vector decomposition to preserve mesh integrity; automated with Python/Bash/SLURM on 16-core HPC cluster

Fabrication: transitioned Blue Tube → phenolic cardboard after warping failure; epoxied centering rings and tube fins; managed BOM/budget; coordinated across recovery and payload subteams; debugged motor igniter slippage at competition
results: Mars Rover: 1,459 ft apogee (within 1,000–2,000 ft spec; simulated 1,676 ft → actual 1,459 ft) — Mach 0.2, 4.22 G peak boost, 12 ft/s descent, 129.7 s total flight; successful rover deployment and recovery
Altitude Rocket: 758 ft apogee — 43 ft from 801 ft target (<6% error; predicted ~757 ft), Mach 0.2, 3.32 G peak boost, 22 ft/s descent, 41.9 s total flight; fully recovered

Ongoing CFD sweep building full Cₗ/Cᴅ/Cₘ aerodynamic database for both configurations; ISA-anchored thermophysics (97,737 Pa / 286.17 K at 1,000 ft for Mars Rover; 99,878 Pa / 287.36 K at 410 ft for Altitude); results feed GNC verification and passive stability validation
Coordinated CAD, simulation, and build across 5-person team; methodology contributed to BNL-level research publication
